text: My name is Anne Defnit, and I'm pleased to present a case of robotic transversus abdominis muscle release for recurrent substernal incisional hernia.
The patient is a seventy-five-year-old male, BMI twenty-seven, who was referred for recurrent substernal incisional hernia.
His past surgical history includes coronary artery bypass grafting in two thousand six, as well as two prior substernal hernia repairs, both onlay repairs with polypropylene mesh.
CT abdomen pelvis was obtained, revealing a seven centimeter defect with herniated colon.
His physical exam mirrored his imaging findings.
The decision was made to proceed to the operating room for robotic retromuscular repair with possible transversus abdominis release.
Three 8mm ports are placed at the level of the anterior superior iliac spine, and the XI da Vinci robotic system is docked for the procedure.
Upon entry into the abdomen, the hernia and its contents are immediately visible, revealing the protrusion of abdominal structures through the defect.
The surgeon begins dissection of the retrorectus space, making an incision in the posterior sheath ten centimeters below the hernia defect and carefully separating it from the underlying muscles.
The surgeon continues the dissection to the contralateral side, crossing the midline within the retrorectus space using robotic assistance.
The dissection reveals the linea alba and the bilateral rectus abdominis muscles, clearly exposing these anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection superiorly towards the hernia defect, using the XI da Vinci robot for precise manipulation within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon carefully dissects the hernia contents free from the defect, leaving the hernia sac densely adherent to the previously placed polypropylene mesh in situ.
The surgeon excises the rectus sheath from the edges of the fascial defect, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon prepares to perform a transversus abdominis muscle release due to the apparent tension observed in the surgical field, indicating a need for additional space for closure.
The surgeon dissects the retrorectus space laterally, exposing the transversus abdominis muscle while carefully preserving the neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon transects the transversus abdominis muscle using electrocautery, exposing the underlying transversalis fascia.
The surgeon carefully dissects the diaphragm free from the transversalis fascia, revealing the anatomical separation between these structures.
The surgeon continues the release of the transversus abdominis muscle inferiorly, ensuring the preservation of all visible neurovascular bundles during the dissection.
Following bilateral transversus abdominis muscle release, the surgeon frees the posterior sheath from the anterior abdominal wall components, revealing the diaphragm.
The surgeon closes the anterior rectus fascial defect using a 2-0 reverse barbed suture in two layers, with visible residual tension, particularly in the superior portion of the closure.
The surgeon closes the anterior fascial defect, incorporating bites of the hernia sac into the suture to prevent future seroma formation.
The surgeon closes the posterior rectus sheath in the midline using a two-oh reverse barbed suture, ensuring there is no tension in the closure.
The surgeon measures the remaining space in the surgical field, which is visibly 18 by 18 centimeters.
The surgeon places a composite mesh, containing PTFE and bioabsorbable components, over the anterior rectus fascial closure to act as a bridge in case of dehiscence due to residual tension.
The surgeon places an eighteen by eighteen centimeter composite mesh into the retro-rectus space and secures it to the anterior abdominal wall at cardinal points using 3-0 suture.
The surgeon completes the procedure by closing the posterior rectus sheath transversely with a two-oh reverse barbed suture.
The patient recovered without a vent and was discharged post-op day one.
CT abdomen pelvis was performed one year post-op or follow-up, revealing an intact repair without recurrence with mesh apparent in the retrorectus space.
